coredata.dc:title: A facile fluorescent sensing strategy for determination of hydrogen peroxide in foods using a nanohybrid of nanoceria and carbon dots based on the target-promoted electron transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shi Gang
Liu, Siyi
Yang, Shujuan
Zhao, Qian
Deng, Jiehong
Shi, Xingbo
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used as disinfectant and bleaching agent in food industry, but excessive amounts of H2O2 residue in foods can cause serious adverse effects on human health. Herein, a fluorescent sensing strategy for rapid detection of H2O2 in foods is described based on a nanohybrid system of nanoceria and carbon dots (CDs). Nanoceria can reduce the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but the presence of H2O2 further quenches the fluorescence remarkably, which is attributed to the H2O2-promoted electron transfer. Based on H2O2 concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching of CDs/nanoceria, the fluorescence method for H2O2 determination is established with a linear range of 0.1–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.047 μM. Meanwhile, the smartphone is utilized as a portable platform for facile and low-cost detection of H2O2, offering a promising method for rapid sensing of H2O2 on site. The fluorescent strategy is applied to H2O2 residue detection in milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet samples successfully, suggesting its great potential for applications in food and agricultural fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensing
Hydrogen peroxide
Nanoceria
Carbon dots
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for fluorescent detection of H2O2
Procedures for H2O2 detection using titanium salt colorimetry
Characterization of nanoceria and carbon dots
Optimization of parameters for H2O2 detection
Analytical performance for H2O2 detection in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) XPS analysis (Ce 3d region) of the nanoceria. Inset of (A) is a high-resolution TEM image. (E) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs (inset shows the photo of CD solution under 365 nm UV lamp illumination). (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs/nanoceria solution in the absence and presence of H2O2 (CDs, 0.32 μg mL−1; nanoceria,75 μg mL−1; H2O2, 100 μM).

(A) Fluorescence spectra against different concentrations of H2O2 ranging from 0 to 1000 μM. (B) Relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔF/F) at 445 nm and H2O2 concentration, and (C) a linear relationship of ΔF/F versus the H2O2 concentration over the range from 0.1 to 100 μM. ΔF = F 0 − F, where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively. (D) Selectivity of the sensing method for H2O2 detection. The concentration of H2O2 is 100 μM and the concentration of other substances are 1000 μM. Sensing system: CDs, 0.16 μg mL−1; nanoceria, 75 μg mL−1; tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2, 10 mM.

(A) Detection of H2O2 using a smartphone. (a) is a photo of the CDs/nanoceria system in the presence of various H2O2 concentration, and (b) is a corresponding gray-scale image. (B) Relationship between V value of HSV color mode and H2O2 concentration. Inset is a linear relationship of (V0 – V) versus the H2O2 concentration over the range from 0.1 to 1 mM, where V0 and V denote the V value in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively. Sensing system: CDs, 0.64 μg mL−1; nanoceria, 75 μg mL−1; tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2, 10 mM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs + H2O2, (c) CDs/nanoceria, and (d) CDs/nanoceria + H2O2 (CDs, 0.32 μg mL−1; nanoceria, 75 μg mL−1; H2O2, 100 μM). (B) TEM image of the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid. (C) Zeta potentials of CDs and CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra and (E) FT-IR spectra of CDs/nanoceria in the absence and presence of H2O2. (F) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of CDs, CDs/nanoceria, and CDs/nanoceria added with H2O2.
Schematic depiction of the fluorescence sensing of H2O2 in foods based on the nanohybrid of CDs and nanoceria.

head.ce:title: A facile fluorescent sensing strategy for determination of hydrogen peroxide in foods using a nanohybrid of nanoceria and carbon dots based on the target-promoted electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used as disinfectant and bleaching agent in food industry, but excessive amounts of H2O2 residue in foods can cause serious adverse effects on human health. Herein, a fluorescent sensing strategy for rapid detection of H2O2 in foods is described based on a nanohybrid system of nanoceria and carbon dots (CDs). Nanoceria can reduce the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but the presence of H2O2 further quenches the fluorescence remarkably, which is attributed to the H2O2-promoted electron transfer. Based on H2O2 concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching of CDs/nanoceria, the fluorescence method for H2O2 determination is established with a linear range of 0.1–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.047 μM. Meanwhile, the smartphone is utilized as a portable platform for facile and low-cost detection of H2O2, offering a promising method for rapid sensing of H2O2 on site. The fluorescent strategy is applied to H2O2 residue detection in milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet samples successfully, suggesting its great potential for applications in food and agricultural fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As a common disinfectant and bleaching agent, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has widespread applications in food industry [1,2]. The harmful microorganisms in food species and food packaging materials can be killed by using H2O2, and thus H2O2 plays an important role in food safety. Nevertheless, the excessive uptake of H2O2 can accelerate the aging process and result in some diseases like cancer for human [3,4], and thereby H2O2 residue in foods may cause serious adverse effects on human health. Accordingly, H2O2 must be eliminated from the finished foods. In China, food laws do not permit any H2O2 residue in food species. Also, the allowance level of residual H2O2 is 0.5 mg L−1 in finished food packages according to the regulations from the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) of the US [5]. Unfortunately, the overuse or abuse of H2O2 occurs frequently. For example, milk added with H2O2 illegally has been often reported in developing countries [6,7]. Residue or illegal addition of H2O2 is a great threat to food safety. As a result, the facile and rapid detection of H2O2 in food species is of very significance.

Up to now, various approaches have been developed for the assay of H2O2 in food science and industry, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [8], electrochemistry [9], electrochemiluminescence [10], chemiluminescence [11], colorimetry [12,13], and so on [14–16]. However, the methods still have some certain disadvantages, such as cumbersome and time-consuming operation, high cost, and use of hazardous reagents. For instance, titanium salt colorimetry, an official method in China for H2O2 detection in foods (GB 5009.226—2016), has a quite time-consuming procedure and needs dangerous reagents. In recent years, fluorescence sensing based on nanomaterials has gained great attention because of its rapid response, sensitivity, and easy implementation of visualization and portable analysis [17–20]. Smartphone as a portable common device recently has been exploited to develop point-of-care detection techniques based on fluorescent and colorimetric assay strategies for a variety of analytes [21–23]. Developing portable techniques for H2O2 detection based on smartphone and fluorescence is highly promising and significant for food safety.

Nanoceria (cerium oxide nanoparticles) has widespread applications in multidisciplinary fields including medicine, chemical sensing, biology, and catalysis because of their favorable catalytic property and low toxicity [24–27]. In recent years, nanoceria is also well known as nanozyme [28–30]. Dual oxidation state oxides of Ce3+ and Ce4+ exist in the nanoceria, which promotes redox reactions on the nanoparticle surface. Nanoceria can mimic activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase which belong to antioxidant enzyme systems [31,32]. Besides, it has been found that nanoceria is an oxidase mimic and it can facilitate oxidation of various substrates which yield colored and fluorescent products [33]. In recent years, based on the oxidase-like property of nanoceria, some analytical methods have been proposed for different analytes due to the quickly catalyzed color reaction [34–36]. In addition, nanoceria is a high band gap semiconductor and thereby it can quench fluorescence of various fluorophores through electron transfer [37–39]. Thanks to the advantage of attractive physicochemical properties, good biocompatibility, and relative inexpensiveness, the development of nanoceria-based chemical and biological sensors has received increasing attention.

Carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots are an emerging type of fluorescent nanocarbons, and possess fascinating properties including stable emission, low cytotoxicity, and good water dispersibility [40–43]. Thus, CDs can be served as a good fluorescent signal supplier for sensing systems [44–46]. Herein, we propose a fluorescent sensing strategy for rapid detection of H2O2 in foods using a nanohybrid of nanoceria and CDs ( Scheme 1).

The sensing strategy is based on a fact that the fluorescence of CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid can be effectively quenched by H2O2. The richness of carboxylate groups and amino groups of CDs and their strong complexation ability to cerium ions make the formation of a nanohybrid of CDs/nanoceria. Nanoceria can reduce the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but the presence of H2O2 further quenches the fluorescence remarkably. According to the linear relationship between H2O2 concentration and fluorescent intensity, the fluorescence method for H2O2 assay is established and used for H2O2 residue detection in milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet samples. Furthermore, the smartphone is utilized as a portable platform for facile and low-cost detection of H2O2, offering a promising method for rapid sensing of H2O2 on-site. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is investigated and attributed to H2O2-promoted electron transfer. The simple and rapid detection method for H2O2 may have great potential for applications in food and agricultural fields.

Citric acid, cerium nitrate hexahydrate, dextran T-10, ethylenediamine, hydrogen peroxide, and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose, titanium dioxide, and serine were supplied by Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were bought from Zhuzhou Xingkonghuabo Co., Ltd. (Zhuzhou, China). Other chemicals with analytical reagent grade were used as obtained without further purification. Milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet samples were purchased from the local supermarket (Changsha, China). High-purity water (18.2 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded exploiting an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 700 V. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterizations were conducted on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by an Escalab 250Xi system (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). Zeta potential measurements and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis were taken on a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained from an XRD-6000 diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected by a UV–vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by an IR Affinity-1 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime decays were obtained from an Edinburgh FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer. An Honor 20s smartphone (Huawei, China) and a light box (black-box UV analyzer) whose light source of 365 nm was fixed at the top (Shanghai Chitang Industrial Co., LTD, China) were utilized to obtain fluorescent photos. A FE20 pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland) was used to determine pH values.

Based on a reported procedure with minor modification [26], a solution containing 10 mL of dextran T-10 (0.1 mg mL−1) and 5 mL of cerium nitrate (0.1 M) was introduced dropwise to a 30 mL of ammonium hydroxide solution (30% wt) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature (25 °C). Subsequently, the dark brown solution was centrifuged twice at 4000 rpm for 30 min to remove any large agglomerates and then the pH of the centrifuged solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 with hydrochloric acid solution (1 M). Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged against an ultrafilter (molecular weight cutoff 30,000) at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained nanoceria was redispersed in water to obtain a 0.75 mg mL−1 nanoceria solution.

CDs were obtained according to a literature method [44]. Typically, a portion of citric acid solution (30 mL, 0.042 g mL−1) and 1608 μL of ethylenediamine were mixed well, and then the mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reactor cooled down naturally, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to settle debris, and then the supernatant was dialyzed against water for 24 h through a dialysis film (molecular weight cutoff 1000). The CD solution (0.064 mg mL−1) with bright blue emission was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescent detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures of fluorescent detection of H2O2 are described briefly as follows. The nanoceria (100 μL, 0.75 mg mL−1) and CDs (250 μL, 64 μg mL−1) were added to 550 μL of tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.2), and the solution was mixed well. Afterwards, 100 μL of H2O2 solution with various concentrations was added into the mixtures. After reaction of 5 min in room temperature (25 °C), the fluorescence spectra from 390 to 600 nm were collected with 355 nm excitation light. For detection of H2O2 by a smartphone, the samples were placed in the light box and radiated by 365 nm light. The fluorescent images were then captured by the smartphone, and HSV (H, hue; S, saturation; V, value) and RGB (R red; G, green; B, blue) color space values were obtained by an application (App, Color Recognizer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for H2O2 detection using titanium salt colorimetry
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures of H2O2 detection using titanium salt colorimetry (GB 5009.226—2016, an official method in China) are described briefly as follows. Firstly, 1.0 g of titanium dioxide and 4.0 g of ammonium sulfate were dissolved in 100 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid, and the mixture was kept heating at 150 °C for 15 h. After the mixture cooled down naturally, it was diluted by 400 mL of water. Then, the solution was filtered by a filter paper and the obtained filtrate was the titanium salt solution used for H2O2 detection. Afterwards, 500 μL of titanium salt solution was introduced to 1.5 mL of water and then 500 μL of H2O2 solution with various concentrations was added. After shaking well and incubation of 10 min, the absorbance at 430 nm was measured.

Milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet were chosen as real samples for H2O2 detection. For the pretreatment of milk, 25 g of milk was put into a 100 mL of volumetric flask, and then 5 mL of zinc acetate solution (0.220 g mL−1) and 5 mL of potassium ferrocyanide solution (0.106 g mL−1) were added to the flask, followed by diluting with water to volume. After shaking well, the mixture was filtered using a filter paper and the filtrate was the pretreated sample. For the pretreatment of dried bean curd and chicken feet samples, an amount of dried bean curd or chicken feet were firstly smashed to uniform small pieces. Subsequently, 10 g of the small pieces was introduced into a 100 mL of volumetric flask, and then zinc acetate solution (5 mL, 0.220 g mL−1) and potassium ferrocyanide solution (5 mL, 0.106 g mL−1) were added to the flask, followed by diluting with water to volume and shaking well. After incubation of 30 min, the mixture was filtered using a filter paper and the filtrate was the pretreated sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanoceria and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis reveals that the nanoceria is well dispersive in water and has an average size of about 1.8 nm ( Fig. 1A and B). The hydrodynamic size of the nanoceria was characterized by DLS technique. As shown in Fig. S1, the average diameter of the nanoceria is about 12 nm which is larger than the size obtained from TEM. The possible reason for the diameter difference is that DLS provides a hydrodynamic size, while TEM reveals the size of dry nanoparticles [47]. High-resolution TEM image reveals that the nanoceria is crystalline (inset of Fig. 1A), which is further identified by XRD pattern (Fig. 1C). XPS spectrum of Ce 3d is shown in Fig. 1D, and the binding energy peaks at 885.0 and 903.2 eV related to cerium(III) and the peaks at 881.5, 899.0, and 916.2 eV corresponding to cerium(IV) can be observed. The results demonstrate that cerium element has a mixed valence state in the nanoceria. FT-IR spectrum of the nanoceria (Fig. S2) shows the presence of characteristic peaks of dextran (for example, 3390 cm−1, 2926 cm−1, and 1793 cm−1 belonging to C-OH, C-H, and C-O-C, respectively), indicating that dextran is an integral coating. The nanoceria solution can keep stability over three months when stored at 4 °C.

Fig. S3A is the TEM image of CDs, and it shows that the CDs possess an average diameter about 2.4 nm (Fig. S3B). As exhibited in Fig. 1E, the CD solution shows bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1E), and the maximal excitation and emission wavelengths are 355 and 450 nm, respectively. The maximal emission wavelength is constant under various excitation lights (Fig. S4), implying a single fluorophore unit of the CDs. FT-IR analysis (Fig. S5) illustrates that the absorption at 1656 cm−1 belonging to CO vibration and the absorption at 3420 cm−1 corresponding to C-OH stretching vibration are present. The peak at 1560 cm−1 related to NH bond indicates the existence of amine groups on the surfaces of CDs.

The above results demonstrate the successful preparation of the nanoceria and the CDs. Next, we investigated the fluorescence of the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid. As depicted in Fig. 1F, the mixture of CDs and nanoceria possesses obvious fluorescence, and interestingly, with the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of the CDs/nanoceria hybrid system is quenched remarkably. In contrast, there is no effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence of sole CDs (Fig. S6). The H2O2-triggered fluorescence quenching provides the possibility for sensitive and rapid detection of H2O2 by using the CDs/nanoceria hybrid system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters for H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain a good analytical performance, the experimental conditions for H2O2 detection were optimized. The fluorescence quenching efficiency [48], ΔF/F 0 (ΔF = F 0 − F, here F 0 and F denote the fluorescence in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively), was utilized to assess the influence of reaction conditions on H2O2 assay. Firstly, the effect of pH on H2O2 detection was investigated, and it can be seen from Fig. S7 that the pH 7.2 (tris-HCl buffer, 10 mM) is more beneficial for the fluorescence quenching and thereby was chosen as the optimum of pH. Next, the response time was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence quenching can be finished within 5 min at room temperature (25 °C) and with the increase of time, the signal has no further obvious change, suggesting a rapid response. Since the fluorescence quenching can be quickly completed at a room temperature (25 °C), the following detection was carried out at the room temperature condition. In addition, the concentration of CDs was optimized, and it can be found from Fig. S9 that the optimum of CDs concentration was 0.16 μg mL−1.

The feasibility of H2O2 detection using the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid was then demonstrated. The linearity and sensitivity were firstly investigated under the optimum experimental conditions. Fig. 2A shows that increasing H2O2 concentration gradually quenches the fluorescence. Fig. 2B illustrates the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio ΔF/F (ΔF = F 0 − F) and the concentration of H2O2, where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively. The ΔF/F has a good linear relationship against the concentration of H2O2 ranging from 0.1 to 100 μM (R 2 = 0.9975) (Fig. 2C). The fitted linear equation can be expressed as: (ΔF/F) = 0.0038 [H2O2] + 0.0218. The limit of detection (LOD) for H2O2 determination was estimated to be 0.047 μM according to 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the background signals). A comparison to previously reported sensors for H2O2 detection is summarized in Table S1, from which it can be found that the sensitivity of the proposed fluorescent strategy is not inferior to those of reported sensors.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed method for H2O2 detection, the effect of several potential interfering substances including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, NO3 -, ClO-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, glucose (Glu), and serine (Ser) were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 2D, there is no remarkable response for these potential interferents, suggesting that the fluorescent method based on the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid can detect H2O2 selectively. The results demonstrate the feasibility for developing a fluorescent sensing platform to detect H2O2 by utilizing the CDs/nanoceria hybrid system.

The visualization of fluorescence variation also inspired us to develop a convenient and portable strategy for H2O2 detection based on a smartphone equipped with a color identification App (Color Recognizer). After the photo of CDs/nanoceria hybrid with adding different concentrations of H2O2 was captured by a smartphone, color digital modes including HSV and RGB color space values were obtained, and the photochrome also could convert to a gray-scale image with better recognition ( Fig. 3A). Since fluorescence peak has no significant change, the HSV mode was chosen to interpret the color in the work. Fig. 3B reveals that the V value is decreased gradually with increasing H2O2 concentration. As shown in inset of Fig. 3B, the (V0 - V) is linearly related to the H2O2 concentration ranging from 0.1 − 1 mM (R 2 = 0.9914), and the fitted linear equation can be indicated as: (V0 - V) = 17.493 [H2O2] + 2.0435. The LOD was calculated to be 32 μM. The smartphone-based detection mode has great potential for rapid and on-site analysis of H2O2 in resource-poor areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for H2O2 detection in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applications of the fluorescent sensing strategy based on the CDs/nanoceria, H2O2 were measured in food samples including milk, dried bean curd, and chicken feet. These samples were also measured by an official method in China (titanium salt colorimetry, GB 5009.226—2016 published by National Health and Family Planning Commission of China). The corresponding detection results are summarized in Table S2. The recovery of H2O2 detection in the range of 90–109% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) in the range of 0.8–4.2% were obtained for the proposed fluorescent strategy, which is not inferior to the official method with recovery ranging from 88% to 116% and RSD ranging from 0.1% to 2.9%. The results indicate good reliability of the developed fluorescent method and great potential in food analysis.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by H2O2 was next explored. Previous researches have been demonstrated that nanoceria can quench fluorescence of various fluorophores like carboxyfluorescein (FAM) and calcein through electron transfer [34–36]. Nanoceria is a high band gap semiconductor, and thus a broad wavelength range of fluorescence emission can be quenched via electron transfer mechanism. When fluorophores are in close proximity to nanoceria, electron transfer from excited fluorophores to nanoceria can take place, resulting in the inhibition of radiative transition of fluorophores. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence of CDs is decreased with the addition of nanoceria, and after the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence quenching is more remarkable. It is known that a mixed valence state including Ce3+ and Ce4+ is present on the surface of nanoceria. H2O2 can adsorb to the nanoceria surface, making Ce3+ transfer to Ce4+ [25], as illustrated in Scheme 1. As a result, the existence of H2O2 may promote the electron transfer from excited CDs to nanoceria, resulting in enhanced fluorescence quenching efficiency. To verify the mechanism of the H2O2-promoted fluorescence quenching, some related experiments were performed.

Fig. 4B and Fig. S10 are the TEM images of CDs/nanoceria, and the TEM analysis reveals that the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid shows a typical multimer morphology. The formation of CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid can be attributed to the richness of carboxylate groups and amino groups of CDs and their strong complexation ability to cerium ions. The zeta potential of CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid is − 17.5 mV, which is higher than that of CDs (− 27.7 mV) (Fig. 4C), further implying the combination of the CDs and nanoceria. Fig. 4D presents the UV–vis absorption of the CDs/nanoceria hybrid in the absence and presence of H2O2, and it can be found that there is an obvious absorption increase in the range of 330–500 nm. However, FT-IR spectra have no significant difference between CDs/nanoceria and CDs/nanoceria added with H2O2 (Fig. 4E). The results indicate that the introduction of H2O2 causes the Ce3+ transfer to Ce4+ on the nanoceria surface and cannot change the emitting units of CDs. A more amount of Ce4+ on the nanoceria surface may promote the electron transfer from excited CDs to nanoceria. To further prove the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime analysis was carried out. As known, fluorescence lifetime would change for electron transfer-caused quenching which belongs to dynamic quenching. As shown in Fig. 4F, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs is 10.50 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs/nanoceria nanohybrid in the absence of H2O2 is 9.93 ns which is obvious larger than that in the presence of H2O2 (8.28 ns). The result supports the fact that electron transfer is accountable for the fluorescence quenching.

In conclusion, a fluorescent sensing strategy for rapid detection of H2O2 in foods has been proposed using a nanohybrid of nanoceria and CDs. H2O2 can remarkably quenches the fluorescence of the CDs/nanoceria system. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to H2O2-promoted electron transfer. The fluorometry for H2O2 detection has a low detection limit. Furthermore, the smartphone can serve as a portable platform for facile and low-cost detection of H2O2 on site. For H2O2 residue detection in real food samples, the fluorescent strategy showed good reliability. The work not only provides a simple and rapid detection method for H2O2 but also expands a new application of nanoceria and CDs to develop chemical sensors.

Shi Gang Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Siyi Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Shujuan Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qian Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiehong Deng: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Xingbo Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 32102074, 31972155, 32001778), Key Research and Development Program of Hunan Province (No. 2021NK2021), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Nos. 2021JJ40237, 2020CR3050, 2019JJ40115), Natural Science Foundation of Changsha (No. kq2014066), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. BX2021099), Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department (No. 19C0897), and Training Program of Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (Nos. kq2106048, kq2009058).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131325.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Invited review: annatto usage and bleaching in dairy foods
J. Dairy Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Kang, R. Campbell, E. Bastian, M. Drake, Invited review: Annatto usage and bleaching in dairy foods, J. Dairy Sci. 93 (2010) 3891–3901.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of sterilization methods for packaging materials used in aseptic packaging systems
Food Bioprod. Process.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Ansari, A. Datta, An overview of sterilization methods for packaging materials used in aseptic packaging systems, Food Bioprod. Process. 81 (2003) 57–65.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of hydrogen peroxide as a causative agent in noodles implicated in food poisoning
J. Food Drug Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.C. Su, S.S. Chou, P.C. Chang, D.F. Hwang, Identification of hydrogen peroxide as a causative agent in noodles implicated in food poisoning, J. Food Drug Anal. 9 (2001) 1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid and selective determination of hydrogen peroxide residues in milk by batch injection analysis with amperometric detection
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.A. Silva, R.H. Montes, E.M. Richter, R.A. Munoz, Rapid and selective determination of hydrogen peroxide residues in milk by batch injection analysis with amperometric detection, Food Chem. 133 (2012) 200–204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric determination of hydrogen peroxide in milk by using a Fenton reaction system
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Abbas, W. Luo, L. Zhu, J. Zou, H. Tang, Fluorometric determination of hydrogen peroxide in milk by using a Fenton reaction system, Food Chem. 120 (2010) 327–331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances on determination of milk adulterants
Food Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots-Hg(II) system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Han, J. Yuan, E. Wang, Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots-Hg(II) system, Anal. Chem. 81 (2009) 5569–5573.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shi Gang Liu is a lecturer in College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, China. He received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University in 2019. His research is focused on the developments of optical sensors and their applications in food safety.
Siyi Liu is a postgraduate student in College of Food Science and Technology at Hunan Agricultural University in Changsha, China. Her major research interest is food analysis.
Shujuan Yang is a postgraduate student in College of Food Science and Technology at Hunan Agricultural University in Changsha, China. Her major research interest is food analysis.

Qian Zhao is an associate professor in College of Food Science and Technology at Hunan Agricultural University in Changsha, China. He received her Ph.D. degree in biology from Tianjin University in 2018. Her research is focused on the developments of biosensors and their applications in food safety.

Jiehong Deng is a professor in College of Food Science and Technology at Hunan Agricultural University in Changsha, China. He received her Ph.D. degree in horticulture from Hunan Agricultural University in 2007. Her current research interests include food processing and food safety.

Xingbo Shi is a professor in College of Food Science and Technology at Hunan Agricultural University in Changsha, China. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Hunan University in 2012. He was a visiting scholarship in University of Florida during 2017–2018, USA. His current research interests include single molecule/nanoparticle fluorescence techniques; biosensors for food safety, such as toxins, heavy metals, illegal additive and development of novel methods to monitor toxic and harmful substance during the food processing. He has published over 30 academic articles.
